behaviors.description: The traveling-salesman heuristic produces a route that is no more than 1.5x the length of the optimal route for inputs up to 20 stops.
behaviors.justification: The TSP heuristic is a local algorithmic function: coordinates in, route order out. To catch a real failure (heuristic produces suboptimal routes beyond 1.5x), we must run the real algorithm with test coordinate sets and verify output quality against known benchmarks. Mocking the heuristic hides the failure. No I/O, external APIs, or module integration required—pure computation.
behaviors.test_description: Test that when the TSP heuristic is given 10–20 coordinate pairs, it produces a route with total distance no more than 1.5× the known optimal (computed exactly for small inputs or verified against benchmarks).

behaviors.description: The delivery time window estimates are computed correctly from the distance between consecutive stops and the configured average speed for the road type.
behaviors.justification: The time calculator is a pure function: distance + speed → time. To catch a real failure (incorrect formula, wrong units, missing road-type config), we run the real calculator with known inputs and verify output. No I/O, no external services, no module integration needed. A mock calculator would pass trivially.
behaviors.test_description: Test that the time estimator correctly computes delivery time for known distance and road-type-specific average speed (e.g., 10 km at 100 km/h highway speed yields 6 minutes).

behaviors.description: The shift-hours validator rejects a route whose total estimated time exceeds the driver's remaining shift hours, returning the excess amount.
behaviors.justification: The validator is a pure comparison function: takes total_time and remaining_hours, returns decision + excess. To catch a real failure (wrong comparison, incorrect excess calculation, off-by-one), we run the real validator with test inputs and verify output. No I/O, no external dependencies, no module wiring needed.
behaviors.test_description: Test that the shift-hours validator correctly rejects routes where estimated total time exceeds remaining shift hours and accurately computes and returns the excess time.

behaviors.description: When one address in the list fails geocoding (API returns no results), the service skips that stop and optimizes the remaining addresses, reporting which addresses were skipped.
behaviors.justification: This behavior spans multiple components: geocoding service (mocked to return no results), filtering logic, and optimization logic. A Unit test of the filter alone would miss critical failures: (1) optimizer not called on filtered list, (2) consolidated items lost during pipeline, (3) reported skipped addresses incorrect. Only an Integration test wiring geocoding mock → filter → optimizer catches that the full pipeline skips correctly, re-optimizes, and reports.
behaviors.test_description: Test that when the geocoding service returns no results for one address in a list of five, the service skips that address, produces an optimized route for the remaining four, and returns a report of which addresses were skipped.

behaviors.description: Duplicate addresses in the input list are consolidated into a single stop with combined delivery items, not visited twice.
behaviors.justification: The invariant 'not visited twice' depends on consolidation (real code) and the optimizer (real code) working together. A Unit test of consolidation alone would miss: (1) consolidated result not passed correctly to optimizer, (2) optimizer bug that still revisits the consolidated location, (3) delivery items lost in consolidation. Only an Integration test (consolidation → optimization → verify route visits each location exactly once) catches real service failures.
behaviors.test_description: Test that when the input address list contains duplicate locations with different delivery items, the service consolidates them into a single stop with combined items and produces a route that visits that location exactly once.
